docs.title: GCSEs for Dads
docs.text:
Clear, simple GCSE revision for busy parents and families.

Built to work properly on phones, stay easy to update, and help people make steady progress without wading through bloated textbooks or awkward PDFs.

docs.title: How these guides work
docs.text:
Each chapter is designed to be quick to scan and easy to revisit: - a short big-picture summary - clear explanations - simple diagrams - quick check-your-understanding questions - links for extra support

Physics access is available now. link to sales site going here..

docs.title: GCSEs for Dads
docs.text:
Clear, simple GCSE revision for busy parents and families.

Built to work properly on phones, stay easy to update, and help people make steady progress without wading through bloated textbooks or awkward PDFs.

docs.title: Start here
docs.text: Pick a subject and begin with the first chapter. Work in short bursts and focus on understanding before memorising.

docs.title: Physics
docs.text:
Choose a topic:

  • Physics 1: Energy
  • Physics 2: Electricity
  • Physics 3: Particle Model of Matter
  • Physics 4: Atomic Structure and Radiation
  • Physics 5: Forces
  • Physics 6: Waves
  • Physics 7: Magnetism and Electromagnetism
  • Physics 8: Space Physics

docs.title: How these guides work
docs.text: Each chapter is designed to be quick to scan and easy to revisit: - a short big-picture summary - clear explanations - simple diagrams - quick check-your-understanding questions - links for extra support

docs.title: GCSEs for Dads – Physics 1: Energy
docs.text:
Don’t worry about reading the formulas now. Just know they’re here at the top if you need them. Scroll down to start.

You don’t need to memorise these formulas. Just know where to find them.

docs.title: Energy Formulas
docs.text: Quantity Formula Meaning Kinetic Energy Ek = ½mv² mass × velocity² ÷ 2 Power P = E / t energy transferred per second Gravitational Potential Energy Ep = mgh mass × gravity × height Elastic Potential Energy Ee = ½ke² spring constant × extension² ÷ 2 Work Done W = F × d force × distance moved Efficiency efficiency = useful energy output / total energy input useful energy ÷ total energy

docs.title: 1. The Big Idea (30 seconds)
docs.text:
• Energy cannot be created or destroyed
  • It only moves between different energy stores

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
A roller coaster starts at the top:

  • Gravitational energy is high
  • As it drops, energy transfers to kinetic energy (movement)
  • Some energy is transferred to thermal energy (lost as heat) due to friction

Key takeaway: Total energy stays the same

docs.title: 2. Energy Stores
docs.text:
Energy exists in different places called stores

  • Kinetic - moving bike
  • Thermal - hot object
  • Gravitational potential - object at height
  • Elastic potential - stretched spring
  • Chemical - food, fuel, batteries
  • Nuclear - atomic energy

Advanced (rarely seen in GCSE)

  • Magnetic
  • Electrostatic

docs.title: 3. Energy Transfers
docs.text:
Energy transfers between stores in four ways

  • Mechanically - pushing an object
  • Electrically - current in a wire
  • Heating - warming objects
  • Radiation - sunlight or infrared

docs.title: 4. Important GCSE Ideas
docs.text:
Energy is conserved

  • Total energy never disappears
  • It only transfers between stores

Energy is often wasted, usually as heat or sound

docs.title: Example: Driving
docs.text: Car engine → movement + heat

docs.title: 6. 15-Minute Learning Mode (watch together)
docs.text:
Watch 3 short videos:

Energy stores and transfers

Kinetic and gravitational energy

Power and efficiency

docs.title: Question 1 – Concept question
docs.text:
A roller coaster starts at the top of a hill and rolls down

Explain why the roller coaster moves faster as it goes downhill

Answer

  • At the top, the roller coaster has gravitational potential energy
  • As it moves down, this energy transfers into kinetic energy
  • Kinetic energy is the energy of movement, so the coaster speeds up

For a bonus point, why does it slow down on the way up?

  • As the coaster goes uphill, kinetic energy is transferred back into gravitational potential energy
  • This reduces movement energy, so it slows down

For another point, why doesn’t it reach the same height?

  • Some energy is transferred to heat and sound due to friction, so less energy remains

docs.title: Question 2 – Real-life application
docs.text:
A car brakes suddenly and the brakes become very hot. Why?

Answer

  • The car has kinetic energy because it is moving
  • Braking creates friction, which slows the car
  • This transfers kinetic energy into thermal energy, heating the brakes

docs.title: GCSEs for Dads – Physics 2: Electricity
docs.text:
Don’t worry about reading the formulas now. Just know they’re here at the top if you need them. Scroll down to start.

You don’t need to memorise these formulas. Just know where to find them.

docs.title: Electricity Formulas
docs.text: Quantity Formula Meaning Charge Q = I × t charge transferred = current × time Voltage V = I × R voltage = current × resistance Power P = V × I electrical power = voltage × current Energy transferred E = P × t energy = power × time

docs.title: Symbols and Units
docs.text: Symbol Meaning Unit Q Charge Coulombs (C) I Current Amps (A) V Voltage Volts (V) R Resistance Ohms (Ω) P Power Watts (W) E Energy Joules (J) t Time Seconds (s)

docs.title: 1. What is Electricity?
docs.text:
Electricity is the flow of electric charge through a complete circuit.

A complete circuit is needed for electricity to flow.

Key parts of a circuit:

  • Power source (cell or battery)
  • Wires
  • Components (lamp, resistor, motor etc.)

docs.title: 2. Electric Charge and Current
docs.text:
Inside metal wires are tiny particles called electrons.

When a battery is connected, it pushes electrons around the circuit.

docs.title: 3. Current
docs.text:
Electric charge flows through a circuit.

Current is the rate of flow of charge.

Unit: Amps (A)

  • A coulomb is a unit used to measure charge
  • 1 amp means 1 coulomb flows each second
  • A coulomb is a large bundle of electrons

Big current means lots of charge moving Small current means little charge moving

Charge equation:

  • Q = I × t

Example:

  • If 2 A flows for 5 seconds:
  • Q = 2 × 5 = 10 C

docs.title: 4. Voltage (Potential Difference)
docs.text:
Voltage is the energy transferred to each unit of charge.

Think of it like pressure pushing electricity through the circuit.

  • Unit: Volts (V)
  • Higher voltage means a stronger push

docs.title: 5. Resistance
docs.text:
Resistance is how much a component opposes the current.

Think of it like narrowing a pipe. - Unit: Ohms (Ω)

Example:

  • Thin wire means high resistance
  • Thick wire means low resistance

docs.title: 6. How they all fit together
docs.text:
Relationship between voltage, current and resistance:

V = I × R

This is Ohm’s Law.

  • Voltage is the push
  • Current is the flow
  • Resistance is the restriction

So:

Push = Flow × Restriction

  • Voltage does not flow
  • Current flows
  • Resistance controls the flow

docs.title: 7.1 Series Circuits
docs.text:
A series circuit has one loop.

Rules:

  • Current is the same everywhere
  • Voltage is shared between components
  • Total resistance = sum of resistances

Example:

  • R₁ = 3Ω
  • R₂ = 2Ω
  • Total resistance = 5Ω

If one component breaks, the whole circuit stops.

docs.title: 7.2 Parallel Circuits
docs.text:
A parallel circuit has multiple paths.

Rules:

  • Voltage is the same across each branch
  • Current splits across branches
  • Resistance decreases when more branches are added

Example:

  • If one lamp breaks, the others still work because each branch is its own circuit
  • This is why homes use parallel circuits

docs.title: 8. Electrical Power
docs.text:
Power is the rate energy is transferred.

  • Unit: Watts (W)

Equation:

  • P = V × I

Example:

  • 230 V appliance drawing 2 A
  • P = 230 × 2 = 460 W

docs.title: 9. Mains Electricity (UK)
docs.text:
Homes use AC electricity.

UK mains supply:

  • 230 V
  • 50 Hz

High voltage can push dangerous currents through the body.

docs.title: 10. Domestic Wiring Safety
docs.text:
Homes use several safety devices.

  • Fuse – melts and breaks the circuit if current gets too high
  • Circuit breaker (MCB) – switches off automatically
  • Earth wire – protects if metal casing becomes live
  • Double insulation – used in appliances like chargers

docs.title: Check your understanding
docs.text:
• What is the unit of current? (Amp, A)
  • What is the unit of resistance? (Ohm, Ω)
  • What does voltage measure? (energy transferred per unit charge)
  • In the water pipe analogy, what does current represent? (flow rate of water)
  • In the analogy, what does voltage represent? (pressure pushing the water)
  • What does resistance represent? (a narrow pipe slowing the flow)
  • If you increase the voltage in a circuit with the same resistance, what happens to the current? (the current increases)
  • If resistance increases, what happens to the current? (the current decreases)
  • Two bulbs are connected in series. What happens if one bulb breaks? (the whole circuit stops working)
  • Why? (because the circuit is no longer complete)
  • Your house lights are wired in parallel. What happens if one bulb blows? (the other lights still work)
  • Why? (each branch has its own path for current)

docs.title: Now watch these
docs.text:
GCSE Physics - Current, Voltage & Resistance

GCSE Physics - Voltage, Current & Resistance (V = IR)

GCSE Physics - Series Circuits

GCSE Physics - Parallel Circuits

docs.title: GCSEs for Dads – Physics 3: Particle Model of Matter
docs.text:
Don’t worry about reading the formulas now. Just know they’re at the top if you need them. Scroll down to start.

You don’t need to memorise these formulas. Just know where to find them.

docs.title: Particle Model Formulas
docs.text: Quantity Formula Meaning Density ρ = m ÷ V density = mass ÷ volume Specific Heat Capacity E = mcΔT energy needed to change temperature Gas Pressure Pressure increases when particles collide more often faster particles = higher pressure

docs.title: Symbols and Units
docs.text: Symbol Meaning Unit E Energy Joules (J) m Mass kg c Specific heat capacity J/kg°C ΔT Temperature change °C ρ Density kg/m³ V Volume m³

docs.title: 1. The Big Idea (30 seconds)
docs.text:
All matter is made of tiny particles.

These particles are always moving.

The way they move explains: - states of matter - temperature - pressure - changes of state

The particle model is one of the most useful ideas in physics.

docs.title: 2. States of Matter
docs.text:
Matter exists in three main states.

State Particle Behaviour Shape Solid particles tightly packed and vibrate fixed shape Liquid particles close but able to slide past each other takes shape of container Gas particles far apart moving fast fills container

Solid particles are: - tightly packed - arranged in a regular structure - vibrating in place

Example: ice, rock, metal.

Solids keep their shape and volume.

Liquid particles are: - still close together - able to move past each other

Liquids: - flow - take the shape of their container - keep the same volume

Example: water.

Gas particles are: - far apart - moving quickly in random directions

Gases: - expand to fill any container - can be compressed easily

Example: air.

docs.title: 3. Density
docs.text:
Density describes how tightly packed particles are.

The equation is:

ρ = m ÷ V

Where: - ρ = density - m = mass - V = volume

Example: A metal block has a mass of 6 kg and a volume of 2 m³

  • ρ = 6 ÷ 2 = 3 kg/m³

Key GCSE idea: - solids usually have higher density because particles are tightly packed - gases have very low density because particles are far apart

docs.title: 4. Changes of State
docs.text:
Matter can change state when energy is added or removed.

Change Direction Melting solid → liquid Freezing liquid → solid Boiling liquid → gas Condensing gas → liquid

The particles do not change, only their movement and spacing.

docs.title: 5. Internal Energy
docs.text:
Internal energy is the total energy inside a substance.

It is made of two things: - kinetic energy - energy of moving particles - potential energy - energy stored due to particle positions

When you heat something: - particles move faster - internal energy increases

Example: Heating water increases the motion of the particles.

docs.title: 6. Specific Heat Capacity
docs.text:
Different substances require different amounts of energy to heat up.

This is measured using specific heat capacity.

The equation is:

E = mcΔT

Where: - E = energy transferred (J) - m = mass (kg) - c = specific heat capacity - ΔT = temperature change (°C)

Example: - heating 1 kg of water by 1°C requires about 4200 J

This is why: - water heats slowly - oceans help stabilise climate

docs.title: 7. Gas Pressure and Temperature
docs.text:
Gas pressure is caused by collisions between gas particles and the container walls.

When particles hit the walls, they produce force.

Pressure depends on: - number of particles - particle speed - volume of container

When temperature increases: - particles move faster - collisions happen more often - pressure increases

Example: A sealed aerosol can becomes dangerous if heated.

Gas pressure is the force per unit area caused by particle collisions with the container walls.

docs.title: 8. Real Life Example
docs.text:
Why does a bike pump get warm when you compress air?

When air is compressed: - particles collide more often - internal energy increases - temperature rises

This is why pumps feel warm.

docs.title: 9. 15-Minute Learning Mode
docs.text:
Watch two short videos:

Particle model of matter: https://youtu.be/zjkBMk5d3tM

Gas pressure and temperature: https://youtu.be/eHizt31t1rs

docs.title: 10. Check Your Understanding
docs.text:
• Why are solids difficult to compress? (particles are tightly packed with almost no gaps)
  • Why can gases be compressed easily? (particles are far apart with large gaps between them)
  • What happens to particle movement when temperature increases? (particles move faster)
  • What happens to gas pressure when temperature increases in a sealed container? (pressure increases because particles collide more often and with more force)
  • What is the equation for density? (ρ = m ÷ V)
  • What two types of energy make up internal energy? (kinetic energy and potential energy)
  • Why does water take a long time to heat up? (it has a high specific heat capacity)
  • What happens to particles during a change of state? (their movement and spacing change, but the particles themselves do not change)

docs.title: Question - Real Life Application
docs.text:
Why should tyre pressure normally be checked when tyres are cold?

Answer:

  • Heating increases the kinetic energy of gas particles.
  • Particles move faster.
  • They collide with the tyre walls more often and with greater force.
  • This increases the pressure.
  • If tyres are checked when hot, the reading will be higher and the tyre may end up under-inflated once it cools.

docs.title: GCSEs for Dads – Physics 4: Atomic Structure and Radiation
docs.text:
Don’t worry about reading the formulas now. Just know they’re here at the top if you need them. Scroll down to start.

You don’t need to memorise these formulas. Just know where to find them.

docs.title: Atomic Structure and Radiation Formulas
docs.text: Quantity Formula Meaning Half-life no fixed equation time taken for half the unstable nuclei to decay Activity counts per second how many decays happen each second Absorbed dose measured in grays energy absorbed by tissue from radiation

docs.title: Symbols and Units
docs.text: Symbol Meaning Unit α Alpha radiation no unit β Beta radiation no unit γ Gamma radiation no unit A Activity Becquerel (Bq) D Absorbed dose Gray (Gy) t½ Half-life time

docs.title: 1. The Big Idea (30 seconds)
docs.text:
• Everything is made of atoms.
  • Atoms are incredibly small but contain even smaller particles.
  • Some nuclei are unstable and give out radiation.
  • This matters in medicine, industry, archaeology and energy.

docs.title: 2. Structure of the Atom
docs.text:
Atoms contain three main particles.

Particle Charge Relative Mass Location Proton +1 1 Nucleus Neutron 0 1 Nucleus Electron -1 Very small Electron shells

docs.title: Important ideas
docs.text:
• The nucleus is tiny but contains most of the mass.
  • Electrons move around the nucleus in energy levels, also called shells.
  • Atoms are mostly empty space.

docs.title: Example: Carbon atom
docs.text:
• 6 protons
  • 6 neutrons
  • 6 electrons

docs.title: 3. How Our Model of the Atom Changed
docs.text: Scientists improved the atomic model over time.

docs.title: Dalton
docs.text: • Atoms were thought to be solid spheres.

docs.title: Thomson
docs.text:
• Discovered electrons.
  • Proposed the plum pudding model.
  • A ball of positive charge with electrons embedded in it.

docs.title: Rutherford
docs.text:
• Used the gold foil experiment.
  • Showed that atoms are mostly empty space.
  • Found that a tiny, dense nucleus exists at the centre.

docs.title: Bohr
docs.text: • Proposed that electrons move in fixed energy levels around the nucleus.

docs.title: Modern model
docs.text:
• Protons and neutrons are in the nucleus.
  • Electrons are in energy levels around the nucleus.

docs.title: 4. Isotopes
docs.text:
Atoms of the same element can have different numbers of neutrons.

These are called isotopes.

docs.title: Example: Carbon isotopes
docs.text: Isotope Protons Neutrons Carbon-12 6 6 Carbon-13 6 7 Carbon-14 6 8

docs.title: Key rule
docs.text:
• Same number of protons = same element
  • Different number of neutrons = different isotope

docs.title: 5. Radioactivity
docs.text:
Some atomic nuclei are unstable.

They break down and release radiation. This process is called radioactive decay.

docs.title: Important facts
docs.text:
• Radioactive decay is random.
  • You cannot predict exactly when one nucleus will decay.
  • You can measure the rate of decay for a large sample.

docs.title: 6. Types of Nuclear Radiation
docs.text: There are three main types.

docs.title: Alpha radiation (α)
docs.text:
Alpha particles contain: - 2 protons - 2 neutrons

This is the same as a helium nucleus.

Properties: - heavy particle - strongly ionising - very short range

Stopped by: - paper - skin

Danger: - not very dangerous outside the body - dangerous if inhaled or swallowed

docs.title: Beta radiation (β)
docs.text:
Beta radiation is a fast electron.

Properties: - medium penetration - medium ionising power

Stopped by: - thin aluminium

docs.title: Gamma radiation (γ)
docs.text:
Gamma rays are electromagnetic waves.

Properties: - no mass - no charge - very penetrating - weakly ionising

Stopped by: - thick lead - thick concrete

docs.title: 7. Half-Life
docs.text:
Radioactive materials decay over time.

Half-life is the time taken for half of the unstable nuclei in a sample to decay.

docs.title: Example starting with 800 atoms
docs.text:
• After one half-life, 400 atoms remain
  • After two half-lives, 200 atoms remain
  • After three half-lives, 100 atoms remain

The amount halves each time.

docs.title: 8. Uses of Radiation
docs.text: Radiation has several useful applications.

docs.title: Medicine
docs.text:
• cancer treatment using radiotherapy
  • medical tracers
  • sterilising equipment

docs.title: Industry
docs.text:
• thickness gauges for paper and metal
  • leak detection in pipes

docs.title: Archaeology
docs.text: • carbon dating to estimate the age of ancient objects

docs.title: 9. Nuclear Safety
docs.text:
Radiation can damage cells and tissue.

Scientists reduce exposure using three main methods:

  • Time: reduce the time spent near the source
  • Distance: stay further from the source
  • Shielding: use dense materials such as lead

docs.title: 10. Check Your Understanding
docs.text:
• What particles are found in the nucleus? (protons and neutrons)
  • What charge does an electron have? (negative)
  • What is an isotope? (atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons)
  • Which type of radiation is the most penetrating? (gamma)
  • Which type of radiation is stopped by paper? (alpha)
  • What is half-life? (the time taken for half the unstable nuclei to decay)
  • Why is alpha radiation especially dangerous inside the body? (because it is strongly ionising)
  • What are the three main ways of reducing radiation exposure? (time, distance and shielding)

docs.title: 11. Common Exam Traps
docs.text:
• Mass number and atomic number are not the same Atomic number = number of protons. Mass number = protons + neutrons.

  • Isotopes are not different elements They are the same element because they have the same number of protons.

  • Radiation is random You cannot predict when one nucleus will decay.

  • Gamma is not a particle It is an electromagnetic wave.

  • Alpha is weak at penetrating but strong at ionising Those are different ideas.

docs.title: 12. Quick Memory Hooks
docs.text:
• atom = tiny structure making up matter
  • nucleus = dense centre
  • isotope = same protons, different neutrons
  • alpha = heavy and strongly ionising
  • beta = medium penetration
  • gamma = most penetrating
  • half-life = halves each time
  • radiation safety = time, distance, shielding

docs.title: 13. Now Watch These
docs.text:
• Atomic Structure
  • Alpha, Beta and Gamma Radiation
  • Radioactive Decay & Half-Life

docs.title: Question 1
docs.text:
Why can alpha radiation be stopped by a sheet of paper?

Answer:

Alpha particles are relatively large and heavy. They collide easily with atoms in the paper, so they lose energy quickly and cannot travel far.

docs.title: Question 2
docs.text:
Smoke detectors use a small radioactive source. Explain how this helps detect smoke.

Answer:

The radioactive source emits alpha radiation, which ionises the air and allows a small electric current to flow. When smoke enters the detector, it absorbs the alpha particles, so the current drops and the alarm is triggered.

docs.title: GCSEs for Dads – Physics 5: Forces
docs.text:
Don’t worry about reading the formulas now. Just know they’re here at the top if you need them. Scroll down to start.

You don’t need to memorise these formulas. Just know where to find them.

docs.title: Forces Formulas
docs.text: Quantity Formula Meaning Weight W = mg weight = mass × gravity Work Done W = F × d force × distance moved Hooke’s Law F = kx force = spring constant × extension Pressure P = F ÷ A force ÷ area Moment moment = F × d turning force = force × distance from pivot Stopping Distance stopping distance = thinking distance + braking distance total distance before a vehicle stops

docs.title: Symbols and Units
docs.text: Symbol Meaning Unit W Weight / Work done Newtons (N) / Joules (J) m Mass kilograms (kg) g Gravitational field strength N/kg F Force Newtons (N) d Distance metres (m) k Spring constant N/m x Extension metres (m) P Pressure Pascals (Pa) A Area m²

docs.title: 1. The Big Idea (30 seconds)
docs.text:
• A force is a push or a pull.
  • Forces can make things speed up, slow down, change direction, or change shape.
  • If forces are balanced, nothing changes.
  • If forces are unbalanced, something accelerates.
  • A lot of GCSE Physics is really about spotting which forces are acting and what the overall effect is.

docs.title: 2. What Is a Force?
docs.text:
A force is a push or a pull that can change how an object moves.

Forces can:

  • start an object moving
  • stop an object moving
  • change the speed of an object
  • change the direction of motion
  • change the shape of an object

Forces are measured in Newtons (N) using a Newton meter.

docs.title: Contact forces
docs.text:
These happen when objects are touching.

Examples:

  • friction: resists movement between surfaces
  • air resistance (drag): friction between an object and air
  • water resistance: drag in water
  • tension: pulling force through a rope or cable
  • normal contact force: support force from a surface

Example: When you push a box across the floor:

  • your push acts forward
  • friction acts backward

docs.title: Non-contact forces
docs.text:
These act without objects touching.

Examples:

  • gravity
  • magnetic force
  • electrostatic force

Example: The Earth pulls objects downward using gravity even though nothing is physically touching them.

docs.title: 4. Weight and Gravity
docs.text:
Gravity is the force that attracts objects with mass toward each other.

On Earth, gravity pulls objects toward the centre of the planet.

Weight is the force caused by gravity acting on a mass.

docs.title: Equation
docs.text:
W = mg

Where:

  • W = weight in Newtons (N)
  • m = mass in kilograms (kg)
  • g = gravitational field strength in N/kg

On Earth, g ≈ 9.8 N/kg In many GCSE questions, this is rounded to 10 N/kg.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
A 10 kg object has a weight of:

W = 10 × 9.8 = 98 N

Mass stays the same wherever you go. Weight changes because gravity changes.

docs.title: 5. Resultant Forces
docs.text:
Objects often have more than one force acting on them.

The resultant force is the overall force after all the forces are combined.

docs.title: Balanced forces
docs.text:
Balanced forces mean:

  • the forces are equal
  • they act in opposite directions
  • the resultant force is zero

Result:

  • the object stays still, or
  • it keeps moving at a constant speed in a straight line

Example: A book resting on a table:

  • weight acts downward
  • support force acts upward

These forces are balanced.

docs.title: Unbalanced forces
docs.text:
Unbalanced forces mean the resultant force is not zero.

Result:

  • the object accelerates
  • speed increases or decreases
  • direction changes

Example: If you push a shopping trolley harder than friction pushes back, it accelerates forward.

docs.title: 6. Work Done
docs.text: Work is done when a force moves an object through a distance.

docs.title: Equation
docs.text:
W = F × d

Where:

  • W = work done in Joules (J)
  • F = force in Newtons (N)
  • d = distance moved in the direction of the force in metres (m)

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
If a force of 20 N pushes a box 5 m:

W = 20 × 5 = 100 J

Work done is energy transferred.

That is a really useful GCSE idea: if work is done, energy has been transferred.

docs.title: 7. Springs and Hooke’s Law
docs.text:
When a force stretches or compresses a spring, it changes length.

The amount it stretches is called the extension.

docs.title: Equation
docs.text:
F = kx

Where:

  • F = force in Newtons (N)
  • k = spring constant in N/m
  • x = extension in metres (m)

The spring constant tells you how stiff the spring is.

  • large k = stiff spring
  • small k = easier to stretch

This rule only works up to the limit of proportionality.

Beyond that point:

  • force and extension are no longer proportional
  • the spring may not return to its original shape

docs.title: 8. Moments and Levers
docs.text:
A moment is the turning effect of a force around a pivot.

Examples include:

  • opening a door
  • using a spanner
  • a seesaw

docs.title: Equation
docs.text:
moment = force × distance

Where:

  • force is measured in Newtons (N)
  • distance is the perpendicular distance from the pivot in metres (m)

The unit of moment is Newton metres (Nm).

docs.title: Key idea
docs.text:
A force has a bigger turning effect when:

  • the force is larger, or
  • it acts further from the pivot

That is why door handles are placed far from the hinges.

docs.title: Principle of moments
docs.text:
For an object in balance:

clockwise moments = anticlockwise moments

docs.title: 9. Pressure in Fluids
docs.text: Pressure happens when a force acts over an area.

docs.title: Equation
docs.text:
P = F ÷ A

Where:

  • P = pressure in Pascals (Pa)
  • F = force in Newtons (N)
  • A = area in m²

docs.title: Key idea
docs.text:
For the same force:

  • smaller area = greater pressure
  • larger area = lower pressure

That is why: - sharp knives cut better - snowshoes stop you sinking as much

docs.title: Pressure in liquids
docs.text:
In liquids, pressure increases with:

  • depth
  • density of the liquid

Example: A diver feels greater pressure the deeper they go.

docs.title: 10. Stopping Distance
docs.text: Stopping distance is the total distance a vehicle travels before it stops.

docs.title: Equation
docs.text: stopping distance = thinking distance + braking distance

docs.title: Thinking distance
docs.text:
This is the distance travelled while the driver reacts.

Typical reaction time is about 0.7 seconds.

Things that increase thinking distance:

  • tiredness
  • alcohol or drugs
  • distractions such as phones

docs.title: Braking distance
docs.text:
This is the distance travelled after the brakes are applied.

Things that increase braking distance:

  • higher speed
  • worn tyres
  • worn brakes
  • wet or icy roads
  • heavier vehicles

docs.title: Important GCSE point
docs.text:
Speed affects stopping distance a lot.

The faster a car is going, the much longer it takes to stop.

docs.title: 11. Check Your Understanding
docs.text:
• What is the difference between contact and non-contact forces? Contact forces need objects to touch. Non-contact forces act at a distance.

  • What does it mean if forces are balanced? They are equal and opposite, so the resultant force is zero.

  • What does the spring constant represent? It shows how stiff a spring is.

  • What is a moment? The turning effect of a force around a pivot.

  • What happens to pressure in a liquid as depth increases? It increases.

  • What two distances make up stopping distance? Thinking distance and braking distance.

docs.title: 12. Common Exam Traps
docs.text:
• Mass and weight are not the same thing Mass is measured in kg. Weight is a force measured in N.

  • Balanced forces do not mean no forces It just means the overall force is zero.

  • Work done only counts if the object moves No movement means no work done.

  • Hooke’s Law does not always apply It only works before the limit of proportionality.

  • Pressure depends on area Same force over a smaller area means bigger pressure.

  • Moment distance must be perpendicular It is not just any distance from the pivot.

docs.title: 13. Quick Memory Hooks
docs.text:
• Force = push or pull
  • Resultant force = overall force
  • Weight = mass × gravity
  • Work done = force × distance
  • Hooke’s Law = force linked to extension
  • Moment = turning effect
  • Pressure = force ÷ area
  • Stopping distance = thinking + braking

docs.title: 14. Now Watch These
docs.text:
• Contact and Non-Contact Forces
  • Hooke’s Law and Springs
  • Stopping Distance

docs.title: GCSEs for Dads – Physics 6: Waves
docs.text:
Don’t worry about reading the formulas now. Just know they’re here at the top if you need them. Scroll down to start.

You don’t need to memorise these formulas. Just know where to find them.

docs.title: Waves Formulas
docs.text: Quantity Formula Meaning Wave speed v = f × λ wave speed = frequency × wavelength Period T = 1 / f time taken for one wave Wave speed v = distance ÷ time used when measuring waves travelling a distance

docs.title: Symbols and Units
docs.text: Symbol Meaning Unit v Wave speed m/s f Frequency Hertz (Hz) λ Wavelength metres (m) T Period seconds (s)

docs.title: 1. The Big Idea (30 seconds)
docs.text:
• A wave transfers energy from one place to another.
  • A wave does not transfer matter overall.
  • In many waves, particles just vibrate around a fixed position.
  • The energy moves through the medium, not the material itself.

docs.title: Think of it like this:
docs.text: If you flick one end of a rope, the shape moves along the rope, but the rope itself does not travel from your hand to the other end.

docs.title: 2. Types of Wave
docs.text: There are two main types you need to know.

docs.title: Transverse waves
docs.text:
• Vibrations are perpendicular to the direction of travel.
  • That means the wave moves one way, while the particles move at right angles to it.
  • Examples include:
  • water surface waves
  • light waves
  • all electromagnetic waves

Key parts: - crest = highest point - trough = lowest point - wavelength = distance from one crest to the next crest

docs.title: Longitudinal waves
docs.text:
• Vibrations are parallel to the direction of travel.
  • That means the particles move back and forth in the same direction the wave travels.
  • Main example: sound waves

Key parts: - compression = particles close together - rarefaction = particles spread apart

docs.title: 3. Wave Properties
docs.text: All waves have a few core properties you need to recognise.

docs.title: Amplitude
docs.text:
• The maximum displacement from the middle position.
  • Bigger amplitude usually means more energy.
  • In sound, bigger amplitude means louder sound.

docs.title: Wavelength (λ)
docs.text:
• The distance between two matching points on a wave.
  • Usually measured crest to crest, trough to trough, or compression to compression.

docs.title: Frequency (f)
docs.text:
• The number of complete waves passing a point each second.
  • Measured in Hertz (Hz).

docs.title: Period (T)
docs.text:
• The time taken for one complete wave.
  • Measured in seconds.

docs.title: Frequency and period relationship
docs.text:
• High frequency means short period.
  • Low frequency means long period.

docs.title: 4. Wave Speed
docs.text: Wave speed tells us how fast a wave travels.

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
v = f × λ

Where: - v = wave speed in m/s - f = frequency in Hz - λ = wavelength in m

docs.title: Worked example
docs.text:
A wave has: - frequency = 4 Hz - wavelength = 2 m

So:

v = f × λ v = 4 × 2 v = 8 m/s

The wave travels 8 metres each second.

docs.title: Typical wave speeds
docs.text:
• sound in air ≈ 340 m/s
  • sound in water ≈ 1500 m/s
  • light in vacuum ≈ 3 × 10⁸ m/s

docs.title: 5. Reflection
docs.text:
Reflection happens when a wave bounces off a surface.

Examples: - light reflecting in a mirror - sound echoing off a cliff or wall

docs.title: Rule of reflection
docs.text:
Angle of incidence = angle of reflection

Important facts: - the direction changes - the frequency stays the same

Reflection is used in: - mirrors - echo sounding - ultrasound imaging

docs.title: 6. Refraction
docs.text:
Refraction happens when a wave enters a different medium and changes speed.

Examples: - light moving from air into water - water waves moving into shallow water

Key facts: - wave speed changes - wavelength changes - frequency stays the same

Because the wave changes speed, it often changes direction too.

A classic example is a straw looking bent in a glass of water. That is light being refracted.

docs.title: 7. Sound Waves
docs.text:
Sound waves are longitudinal waves.

They can travel through: - solids - liquids - gases

They cannot travel through a vacuum because there are no particles to vibrate.

docs.title: Speed of sound
docs.text: Sound travels: - slowest in gases - faster in liquids - fastest in solids

docs.title: Sound wave properties
docs.text:
• amplitude links to loudness
  • frequency links to pitch

docs.title: Real life example
docs.text: You see lightning before you hear thunder because light travels much faster than sound.

docs.title: 8. Check Your Understanding
docs.text:
• What does a wave transfer? (energy)
  • Why does a wave not transfer matter overall? (the particles only vibrate or oscillate around fixed positions)
  • What is the difference between transverse and longitudinal waves? (transverse waves vibrate at right angles to the direction of travel, longitudinal waves vibrate in the same direction)
  • What does wavelength measure? (the distance between two matching points on a wave)
  • What does frequency measure? (the number of complete waves passing a point each second)
  • What happens to wavelength when a wave enters a different medium? (it changes if the wave speed changes)
  • Why can sound not travel through space? (there are no particles in a vacuum to vibrate)
  • What does amplitude tell you about a sound wave? (its loudness)
  • What does frequency tell you about a sound wave? (its pitch)

docs.title: 9. Quick Memory Hooks
docs.text:
• wave = energy moves
  • transverse = vibrations at right angles
  • longitudinal = vibrations in the same direction
  • amplitude = height / energy
  • frequency = waves per second
  • wavelength = length of one wave
  • reflection = bounce
  • refraction = change of speed and direction

docs.title: 10. Common Traps
docs.text:
• Thinking waves carry matter from one place to another
  • Mixing up wavelength and amplitude
  • Forgetting that frequency stays the same during refraction
  • Forgetting that sound is longitudinal
  • Forgetting that sound cannot travel through a vacuum

docs.title: 11. Now Watch These
docs.text:
• Transverse and Longitudinal Waves
  • Sound Waves

docs.title: GCSEs for Dads – Physics 7: Magnetism and Electromagnetism
docs.text:
Don’t worry about reading the formulas now. Just know they’re here at the top if you need them. Scroll down to start.

You don’t need to memorise these formulas. Just know where to find them.

docs.title: Magnetism and Electromagnetism Formulas
docs.text: Quantity Formula Meaning Magnetic force on a current-carrying wire F = B × I × L force on a wire in a magnetic field Magnetic flux density B = F ÷ (I × L) magnetic field strength calculated from force, current and wire length

docs.title: Symbols and Units
docs.text: Symbol Meaning Unit F Force Newton (N) B Magnetic flux density Tesla (T) I Current Ampere (A) L Length of wire in magnetic field metres (m)

docs.title: 1. The Big Idea (30 seconds)
docs.text:
• Magnets create magnetic fields.
  • Magnetic fields can push or pull magnetic materials and other magnets.
  • Electric current also creates magnetic fields.
  • A coil of wire can be turned into an electromagnet.
  • If a current-carrying wire sits in a magnetic field, it feels a force.
  • This is the basis of motors and loudspeakers.

docs.title: 2. Magnetic Fields
docs.text: A magnetic field is the region around a magnet where magnetic forces act.

docs.title: Key ideas
docs.text:
• Magnets have two poles: north and south.
  • Opposite poles attract.
  • Like poles repel.
  • Magnetic forces act at a distance without touching.
  • Magnetic fields are strongest at the poles.

docs.title: Magnetic field lines
docs.text:
Magnetic fields are usually shown using field lines.

Important properties of magnetic field lines:

  • They run from north pole to south pole.
  • They never cross.
  • The closer the lines are, the stronger the field.

The field is strongest where the lines are closest together, usually at the poles.

Real-life example: The Earth has a magnetic field, which is why a compass needle points north.

docs.title: 3. Magnetic Materials
docs.text: Some materials are magnetic and can be attracted by magnets.

docs.title: Examples of magnetic materials
docs.text:
• iron
  • steel
  • nickel
  • cobalt

docs.title: Permanent magnets
docs.text:
• Produce their own magnetic field.
  • Do not easily lose their magnetism.

docs.title: Induced magnets
docs.text:
• Become magnetic when placed in a magnetic field.
  • Lose magnetism when the field is removed.

Soft iron magnetises easily, but also loses its magnetism easily. That makes it useful in electromagnets.

Real-life example: A paperclip can become magnetised when placed near a strong magnet.

docs.title: 4. Magnetic Fields Around a Current-Carrying Wire
docs.text:
When electric current flows through a wire, it creates a magnetic field around the wire.

The field lines form circles around the wire.

docs.title: Right-hand grip rule
docs.text:
Use the right-hand grip rule to work out the field direction:

  • Thumb points in the direction of the current.
  • Fingers curl in the direction of the magnetic field.

Key idea: Electric current always produces a magnetic field.

Real-life example: This effect is used inside electromagnets and motors.

docs.title: 5. The Solenoid
docs.text:
A solenoid is a long coil of wire carrying an electric current.

When current flows through it, it produces a magnetic field like a bar magnet.

docs.title: Properties of a solenoid
docs.text:
• It has a north and south pole.
  • The magnetic field inside the coil is strong.
  • The field inside is also uniform.
  • The field outside is weaker.

A uniform magnetic field means the field strength is the same all the way through that region.

docs.title: Ways to increase the strength of a solenoid
docs.text:
• Increase the current.
  • Add more turns of wire.
  • Insert an iron core.

Real-life example: Solenoids are used in relays and electric door locks.

docs.title: 6. Electromagnets
docs.text:
An electromagnet is a magnet made by current flowing through a coil of wire around an iron core.

Electromagnets are useful because:

  • they can be switched on and off
  • their strength can be changed

docs.title: Ways to increase the strength of an electromagnet
docs.text:
• Increase the current.
  • Increase the number of turns in the coil.
  • Use a soft iron core.

Real-life example: Scrapyard cranes use electromagnets to lift steel.

docs.title: 7. The Motor Effect
docs.text:
When a current-carrying wire is placed in a magnetic field, a force acts on the wire.

This is called the motor effect.

docs.title: Fleming’s Left-Hand Rule
docs.text:
Use Fleming’s Left-Hand Rule to work out the direction of the force:

  • First finger = magnetic field
  • Second finger = current
  • Thumb = force or motion

docs.title: The force gets bigger when
docs.text:
• the current is larger
  • the magnetic field is stronger
  • more wire is inside the magnetic field

Real-life example: Electric motors use the motor effect to create movement.

docs.title: 8. Electric Motors
docs.text: Electric motors convert electrical energy into kinetic energy.

docs.title: How a simple motor works
docs.text:
• A current flows through a coil inside a magnetic field.
  • Forces act on opposite sides of the coil.
  • The forces act in opposite directions.
  • This makes the coil rotate.
  • A split-ring commutator reverses the current every half turn.
  • This keeps the coil turning in the same direction.

Real-life example: Electric motors are used in fans, washing machines and electric cars.

docs.title: 9. Loudspeakers
docs.text: Loudspeakers use the motor effect to make sound.

docs.title: How a loudspeaker works
docs.text:
• An alternating current flows through a coil.
  • The coil sits in a magnetic field.
  • The current keeps changing direction.
  • This makes the coil move backwards and forwards.
  • The coil is attached to a cone.
  • The cone vibrates and makes sound waves in the air.

Real-life example: Headphones, phone speakers and hi-fi speakers all work using this idea.

docs.title: 10. Check your understanding
docs.text:
• Why are magnetic fields strongest at the poles of a magnet?
  • How is an induced magnet different from a permanent magnet?
  • Why are the field lines around a straight wire circular?
  • What does the right-hand grip rule tell you?
  • Why does adding an iron core make a solenoid stronger?
  • What is the motor effect in one sentence?
  • What do the three fingers represent in Fleming’s Left-Hand Rule?
  • Why does a motor need a split-ring commutator?
  • Why does a loudspeaker cone move backwards and forwards instead of in one direction?

docs.title: 11. Common mistakes and quick fixes
docs.text:
• Mixing up attraction and repulsion Opposite poles attract, like poles repel.

  • Forgetting that current creates magnetism A current-carrying wire always produces a magnetic field.

  • Confusing right-hand and left-hand rules Right hand grip rule is for magnetic field around a wire. Fleming’s Left-Hand Rule is for force in the motor effect.

  • Forgetting how to strengthen an electromagnet Think: more current, more turns, iron core.

  • Thinking the motor just keeps spinning automatically The commutator is needed to reverse the current every half turn.

docs.title: 12. YouTube links
docs.text:
• Magnets
  • Electromagnetism

docs.title: GCSEs for Dads – Physics 8: Space Physics
docs.text:
Don’t worry about reading the formulas now. Just know they’re here at the top if you need them. Scroll down to start.

You don’t need to memorise these formulas. Just know where to find them.

docs.title: Space Physics Formulas
docs.text: Quantity Formula Meaning Orbital speed v = 2πr ÷ T orbital speed = circumference of orbit ÷ time for one orbit Distance distance = speed × time distance travelled = speed multiplied by time Red shift relationship Greater red shift = faster recession the more a galaxy’s light is red-shifted, the faster it is moving away

docs.title: Symbols and Units
docs.text: Symbol Meaning Unit v Speed metres per second (m/s) r Radius of orbit metres (m) T Time period seconds (s) π Pi no unit

docs.title: 1. The Big Idea (30 seconds)
docs.text:
• Space physics explains where Earth fits in the solar system, galaxy and universe.
  • It helps explain why planets orbit, how satellites work, and why we get seasons.
  • It also explains how stars form, change over time, and eventually die.
  • At the biggest scale, it helps scientists understand how the universe began and how it is changing.

docs.title: 2. The Solar System
docs.text:
The solar system consists of the Sun, planets, dwarf planets, moons, asteroids and comets.

  • The Sun is at the centre of the solar system.
  • The eight planets orbit the Sun in roughly circular paths.

  • The planets, in order from the Sun, are:

  • Mercury

  • Venus
  • Earth
  • Mars
  • Jupiter
  • Saturn
  • Uranus

  • Neptune

  • The inner planets are rocky.

  • The outer planets are much larger and are mostly made of gas or ice.

docs.title: Quick facts
docs.text:
• Earth orbits the Sun once every year.
  • The Moon orbits Earth about once every month.

docs.title: 3. Orbital Motion
docs.text:
An orbit happens when one object moves around another because of gravity.

Gravity provides the force that keeps planets, moons and satellites in orbit.

Without gravity, an object would move off in a straight line.

In a circular orbit:

  • gravity pulls the object towards the centre
  • the object is already moving forwards
  • this combination makes it travel in a curved path

docs.title: Key ideas
docs.text:
• Bigger gravitational force can keep objects in tighter or faster orbits.
  • Objects closer to a planet or star usually orbit faster.
  • The further away a planet is from the Sun, the longer its year tends to be.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• The Moon stays in orbit around Earth because Earth’s gravity pulls it inward.
  • An artificial satellite stays in orbit for the same reason.

docs.title: 4. Satellites
docs.text:
A satellite is any object that orbits a planet or star.

There are two types:

  • Natural satellites - these occur naturally
  • Artificial satellites - these are made by humans and placed into orbit

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• The Moon is a natural satellite of Earth.
  • A GPS satellite is an artificial satellite.

docs.title: Artificial satellites are used for:
docs.text:
• communication
  • weather forecasting
  • GPS and navigation
  • TV signals
  • observing Earth
  • space research

Different satellites orbit at different heights depending on their job.

  • Low orbit satellites are often used for imaging and observation.
  • Higher orbit satellites are often used for communication.

docs.title: Real-life examples
docs.text:
• Weather satellites can take images of cloud movement.
  • GPS satellites help phones and cars work out location.

docs.title: 5. The Life Cycle of a Star
docs.text: Stars form from clouds of dust and gas called nebulae.

docs.title: How a star begins
docs.text:
1. Gravity pulls gas and dust together.
  2. As the cloud contracts, it gets hotter.
  3. Eventually the centre becomes hot enough for nuclear fusion to begin.
  4. A star is born.

Most of a star’s life is spent in the main sequence stage.

During this stage:

  • hydrogen nuclei fuse to form helium
  • huge amounts of energy are released

What happens next depends on the size of the star.

docs.title: 5.1 Life cycle of a star like the Sun
docs.text:
• Nebula
  • Protostar
  • Main sequence star
  • Red giant
  • White dwarf
  • Black dwarf

A star like the Sun eventually runs out of hydrogen fuel.

  • It expands into a red giant.
  • Its outer layers drift away.
  • The hot core left behind becomes a white dwarf.
  • Over a very long time it cools and becomes a black dwarf.

docs.title: 5.2 Life cycle of a massive star
docs.text:
• Nebula
  • Protostar
  • Main sequence star
  • Red supergiant
  • Supernova
  • Neutron star or black hole

A much more massive star becomes a red supergiant.

  • It may then explode in a supernova.
  • This explosion spreads heavy elements into space.
  • The remains can become a neutron star or, if the core is massive enough, a black hole.

docs.title: 6. Red Shift
docs.text:
Light from distant galaxies can be analysed.

Scientists look at spectral lines in that light.

If the spectral lines are shifted towards the red end of the spectrum, this is called red shift.

docs.title: What red shift tells us
docs.text:
• Red shift shows that a galaxy is moving away from us.
  • The bigger the red shift, the faster the galaxy is moving away.

This is important evidence that the universe is expanding.

docs.title: Helpful comparison
docs.text: It is a bit like how the sound of a vehicle changes as it moves away, except this is light rather than sound.

docs.title: 7. The Expanding Universe
docs.text:
Observations show that most distant galaxies are moving away from us.

This suggests that space itself is stretching.

The universe began from a very hot, dense state.

This idea is called the Big Bang theory.

Over time, the universe has expanded and cooled.

docs.title: Evidence for the Big Bang
docs.text:
• red shift from distant galaxies
  • microwave radiation filling the universe
  • the relative amounts of light elements such as hydrogen and helium

docs.title: Important GCSE point
docs.text:
The universe is not expanding into empty space.

It is space itself that is expanding.

docs.title: 8. Simple Comparison to Remember
docs.text:
• Solar system = planets orbiting the Sun
  • Galaxy = a huge collection of stars held together by gravity
  • Universe = everything that exists

Our solar system is part of the Milky Way galaxy.

The Milky Way is just one of billions of galaxies in the universe.

docs.title: 9. Check Your Understanding
docs.text:
• What is at the centre of our solar system?
  • What force keeps planets and satellites in orbit?
  • What is the difference between a natural satellite and an artificial satellite?
  • Why would an orbiting object move in a straight line if gravity was removed?
  • What stage does a star spend most of its life in?
  • What does hydrogen fuse into inside a star?
  • What happens to a star like the Sun after the main sequence stage?
  • What is a supernova?
  • What does red shift tell us about a galaxy?
  • What does a bigger red shift mean?
  • What is the evidence that the universe is expanding?
  • What is the difference between a solar system, a galaxy and the universe?

docs.title: 10. Answers
docs.text:
1. The Sun.
  2. Gravity.
  3. A natural satellite occurs naturally, like the Moon. An artificial satellite is made by humans and placed into orbit.
  4. Because without gravity there would be no inward force to keep changing its direction.
  5. The main sequence stage.
  6. Helium.
  7. It expands into a red giant, then becomes a white dwarf, and eventually a black dwarf.
  8. A massive explosion of a star at the end of its life.
  9. That it is moving away from us.
  10. The galaxy is moving away faster.
  11. Red shift of light from distant galaxies, microwave background radiation, and the relative amounts of light elements.
  12. A solar system is planets orbiting a star, a galaxy is a huge group of stars held together by gravity, and the universe is everything that exists.

docs.title: 11. Now Watch These
docs.text:
• The Sun, Stars and Universe
  • Lifecycle of Stars
  • Red Shift

docs.title: GCSEs for Dads – Physics 1: Energy
docs.text:
Don’t worry about reading the formulas now. Just know they’re here at the top if you need them. Scroll down to start.

You don’t need to memorise these formulas. Just know where to find them.

docs.title: Formulas
docs.text: Quantity Formula Meaning

docs.title: Symbols and Units
docs.text: Symbol Meaning Unit

docs.title: 1. The Big Idea (30 seconds)
docs.text:
• Energy cannot be created or destroyed
  • It only moves between different energy stores

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
A roller coaster starts at the top:

  • Gravitational energy is high
  • As it drops, energy transfers to kinetic energy (movement)
  • Some energy is transferred to thermal energy (lost as heat) due to friction

Key takeaway: Total energy stays the same

docs.title: 2. Energy Stores
docs.text:
Energy exists in different places called stores

  • Kinetic - moving bike
  • Thermal - hot object
  • Gravitational potential - object at height
  • Elastic potential - stretched spring
  • Chemical - food, fuel, batteries
  • Nuclear - atomic energy

Advanced (rarely seen in GCSE)

  • Magnetic
  • Electrostatic

docs.title: 3. Energy Transfers
docs.text:
Energy transfers between stores in four ways

  • Mechanically - pushing an object
  • Electrically - current in a wire
  • Heating - warming objects
  • Radiation - sunlight or infrared

docs.title: 4. Important GCSE Ideas
docs.text:
Energy is conserved

  • Total energy never disappears
  • It only transfers between stores

Energy is often wasted, usually as heat or sound

docs.title: Example: Driving
docs.text: Car engine → movement + heat

docs.title: Check your understanding
docs.text:
• What is the unit of current? (Amp, A)
  • What is the unit of resistance? (Ohm, Ω)
  • What does voltage measure? (energy transferred per unit charge)
  • In the water pipe analogy, what does current represent? (flow rate of water)
  • In the analogy, what does voltage represent? (pressure pushing the water)
  • What does resistance represent? (a narrow pipe slowing the flow)
  • If you increase the voltage in a circuit with the same resistance, what happens to the current? (the current increases)
  • If resistance increases, what happens to the current? (the current decreases)
  • Two bulbs are connected in series. What happens if one bulb breaks? (the whole circuit stops working)
  • Why? (because the circuit is no longer complete)
  • Your house lights are wired in parallel. What happens if one bulb blows? (the other lights still work)
  • Why? (each branch has its own path for current)

docs.title: Now watch these
docs.text:
GCSE Physics - Current, Voltage & Resistance

GCSE Physics - Voltage, Current & Resistance (V = IR)

GCSE Physics - Series Circuits

GCSE Physics - Parallel Circuits
